value: Headline: The June 19 WPSR's 6.1 mb crude draw to 412.1 mb did the heavy lifting, but Cushing remains the story — stocks fell another 1.1 mb to 18.96 mb, firmly below the ~25 mb operational floor and operating on tank bottoms. WTI settled at $70.22 on Monday, +$0.99 on the session, as the market continues to digest a tight US balance against a still-fragile Hormuz backdrop. With runs at 96.1% utilization and exports pulling barrels out of an already-stressed PADD 2 hub, the front of the curve has every reason to stay firm. Crude & products: For the week ending June 19, 2026, the latest EIA report showed commercial crude drawing 6.1 mb to 412.1 mb, the product of high run rates and steady export pull — 4-week crude exports ran 4.93 mb/d against 5.75 mb/d imports. Refinery utilization eased 0.6 pts to 96.1% with crude inputs at 17.11 mb/d, keeping the system's demand pull strong into peak summer. Products diverged: gasoline built 2.1 mb to 216.3 mb and distillate built 3.0 mb to 106.1 mb, with jet up 1.3 mb to 46.3 mb. The Cushing draw of 1.1 mb to 18.96 mb is the figure that matters most — sub-floor stocks deepen deliverability stress and set up basis volatility at the NYMEX delivery point. Refining margins: Margins held firm into Monday's settle, with the 3:2:1 crack at $54.68/bbl. The product split favored the middle of the barrel — the distillate crack sat at $62.38 against a gasoline crack of $50.83, with jet lagging at $34.96. RBOB settled at $2.8821/gal (+$0.06) and ULSD at $3.1572/gal (+$0.06), both tracking the crude move higher. The distillate build of 3.0 mb tempers some of the diesel-crack enthusiasm, but at $62.38 the middle distillate remains the place refiners want to lean their yield. Curve structure: The WTI curve stays in backwardation, with M1–M2 at $0.31 and M1–M12 at $3.36, consistent with a tight prompt and the sub-floor Cushing picture. Brent's structure is flatter — M1–M2 at $0.10 and M1–M12 at $2.06 — leaving the Brent–WTI spread at a narrow $3.26. That narrow spread is the watch item: with WTI this close to Brent, the US export arb weakens, and softer export pull would let barrels back up domestically — a pressure valve on the Cushing stress only if WTI eases enough to re-widen the spread back toward export economics. News & geopolitics: The supply-side narrative is dominated by the Iran war fallout — the IEA now sees global supply plunging below demand this year, and oil-gear maker NOV cut earnings guidance citing higher costs and snarled deliveries from the conflict. Ukraine's refinery strikes have pushed Russia into a deepening fuel crisis, with Putin himself acknowledging growing gasoline shortages — a bullish wrinkle for global product cracks. Traders are weighing a 'fragile Hormuz truce' as flows reroute, with Bousso framing the calm as masking a chaotic rebalancing ahead. ING argues prices have overshot to the downside, while a separate thesis sees the next rally driven by stockpile buying — both point to upside risk against a backdrop where Pakistan eyes cheaper Iranian crude and Libya's barrels re-enter the Western calculus. For today, the tape leans constructive: tight US inventories, a sub-floor Cushing, and a supply-deficit IEA call all argue the path of least resistance is higher.
